coredata.dc:description: Brain disorders, encompassing neurodegenerative conditions and intracranial neoplasms, present formidable obstacles in the realm of pharmacological delivery due to the existence of athe blood–brain barrier (BBB) and the restricted bioavailability of therapeutic agents. Alginate-derived nanoformulations have emerged as highly promising systems for drug delivery, offering attributes such as biocompatibility, regulated release, and improved targeting efficacies. This review investigates contemporary advancements in alginate-based nanoformulations, with a particular emphasis on their efficacy in surmounting obstacles to successful pharmacological delivery to the brain. Initially, we furnish a comprehensive overview of alginate, underscoring its pertinent properties, biomedical applications, and inherent limitations. Subsequently, the discourse progresses to strategies for nanoformulation, which encompass lipid-based, polymeric, and inorganic methodologies, with a focus on their benefits in relation to cerebral targeting. Moreover, this review entails the therapeutic potential of alginate-based nanoformulations in addressing significant neurological disorders, including Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, brain tumours, traumatic brain injury, epilepsy, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. By amalgamating cutting-edge nanotechnology with the distinctive properties of alginate, these formulations signify a promising pathway for the advancement of efficacious therapies aimed at brain targeting. Additionally, prospective research trajectories and challenges associated with the optimization of alginate-based nanocarriers for clinical applications are also elucidated.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Brain disorders, encompassing neurodegenerative conditions and intracranial neoplasms, present formidable obstacles in the realm of pharmacological delivery due to the existence of athe blood–brain barrier (BBB) and the restricted bioavailability of therapeutic agents. Alginate-derived nanoformulations have emerged as highly promising systems for drug delivery, offering attributes such as biocompatibility, regulated release, and improved targeting efficacies. This review investigates contemporary advancements in alginate-based nanoformulations, with a particular emphasis on their efficacy in surmounting obstacles to successful pharmacological delivery to the brain. Initially, we furnish a comprehensive overview of alginate, underscoring its pertinent properties, biomedical applications, and inherent limitations. Subsequently, the discourse progresses to strategies for nanoformulation, which encompass lipid-based, polymeric, and inorganic methodologies, with a focus on their benefits in relation to cerebral targeting. Moreover, this review entails the therapeutic potential of alginate-based nanoformulations in addressing significant neurological disorders, including Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, brain tumours, traumatic brain injury, epilepsy, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. By amalgamating cutting-edge nanotechnology with the distinctive properties of alginate, these formulations signify a promising pathway for the advancement of efficacious therapies aimed at brain targeting. Additionally, prospective research trajectories and challenges associated with the optimization of alginate-based nanocarriers for clinical applications are also elucidated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Brain disorders may be defined as a progressive degeneration, damage, loss of neurons, dysfunction or impairment of cognitive functions (Feng et al., 2023; Gadhave et al., 2024; Lan et al., 2024; Liao et al., 2024). Ageing people are the suffering population in neurodegenerative disorders which poses a significant challenge globally (Riccardi et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2024; Wilson et al., 2023). Irrespective of endless efforts of researchers equipped with advanced instrumentation and medicinal possibilities, the outcome has not been favourable to date. Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, trauma injury, brain tumour, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, epilepsy, intracerebral hemorrhage are most common brain disorders (Hu et al., 2023; Huang et al., 2024; Lamptey et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021a; Zhang et al., 2024b). Among all brain disorders, brain tumour is considered as one of the deadliest diseases owing to its intricate nature impacting the health of people significantly throughout the globe (Gunasundari and Selva Bhuvaneswari, 2025; Ijaz et al., 2025; Ji et al., 2022; Khalighi et al., 2024). An early-stage detection and visualization of brain tumours is not possible due to lack of details provided by conventional techniques used for its detection (Hasan et al., 2023; Ijaz et al., 2025). On the basis of proliferation rate and life threating capacity, WHO has classified brain tumours from grade 1 to 4(Rasheed et al., 2021). To find a suitable and effective alternative for curing or reversing of such perturbations in brain, nanomedicines have potential to be used as active negotiators for drug delivery across the blood brain barrier. In recent years, considerable attention has been given for the treatment of neuro disorders by using nano-based drugs as nanoformulations offers a holistic medicinal approach because of extraordinary properties of nanoformulations like biodegradability, biocompatibility and innocuous nature (Venkatesan et al., 2017). Nanoformulations have revolutionised the realm of drug delivery into the brain owing to their vital applications and ease of use (Jadhav et al., 2024). However, the development of economic and non-toxic nanoformulations is still a bottle neck for the researchers (Patra et al., 2018). In this field, nanodrugs derived from natural systems such as alginate have propelled the drug delivery systems to advanced level to combat with the shortcomings of conventional drug delivery systems (Karim et al., 2022). Alginate is a most extensively used polysaccharide derived naturally from brown algae and bacteria (Abasalizadeh et al., 2020; Singh et al., 2022), which is used in drug delivery systems due to its number of excellent properties like biocompatibility, low toxicity, high absorption without undergoing any chemical change inside the body (Froelich et al., 2023). Numerous studies revealed that alginate based nanoformulations is one of the hotspots for developing the novel nano drug carriers across the blood brain barrier (Aziz et al., 2023; Bayir, 2023; Spadari et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2021b). Alginate nanoformulations have emerged as a safe and efficient tool for drug delivery and treatment of brain disorders because of their convenience of preparation and can be administered by multiple routes as per the requirement (Eleftheriadou et al., 2022; Hariyadi and Islam, 2020). Moreover, extraordinary stability, biocompatibility, excellent drug loading capacity, strong ligand affinity further makes alginates nanoformulations excellent drug delivery medium which can deal with other physical variables also including pH, temperature etc. (Hamid Reza and Seyed Morteza, 2023).

The conjunction of versatile properties of alginate and excellent therapeutic efficacy of nanoformaulations have transformed the modern medicine systems used for the treatment of various brain disorders and neurosurgeries (Rahman et al., 2022; Sood et al., 2021). Compared to traditional medicines, the nano alginates offer a seamless synergy of propitious nanovehicles with effective drug delivery across the blood brain barrier. Alginate nanoformulations are potential candidates for translocation of nanodrugs to targeted area of brain and has provided an excellent platform that has overcome the biochemical barriers during drug delivery (Khan et al., 2018). Herein, we have reviewed the various natural source of occurrence of alginates, their coalescence with nanomedicines along with their versatile biomedical applications with special reference to drug delivery systems in neurological disorders yoking unique structural features and different forms of nanoformulations. The present review recapitulates the deep study of effective use of nanotechnology and engrossment of alginates in the realm of nanomedicines. The review casts light on the different types of nanoformulations being used in brain drug delivery systems and how alginates based nanoformulations have unlocked the drug delivery to inaccessible areas of brain to treat deadly neurodegerative diseases. Also, the nano drug biodistribution, accumulation, circulation duration and mode of administration are discussed. Knowledge of fate of nanodrugs once administered by any means and selection of type nanoformulations has been put in a nutshell to make it worthy for readers and researchers. We have also included the critical points of their shortcomings, challenges that must be take into account for future research roadmap are summarized.

In 1881, alginate was discovered and extracted by E.C.C. Stanford from kelp while looking for some useful harvests (Nøkling-Eide et al., 2023). But applications were explored after a long period by Thornley in 1923(Tordi et al., 2024) and documented by FDA as a safe product in food industry and pharmaceutical field in 1970 (Bojorges et al., 2023). For last few decades, the quest for naturally extracted derivatives that are eco-conscious as well as pharmaceutically suitable with higher suitability at cellular level have been the main focus of researchers (Distler et al., 2020; Gheorghita Puscaselu et al., 2020; Hasnain et al., 2020). Amongst which, alginate has emerged as potential candidate which are nontoxic, non-irritant, edible, recyclable and hold exceptional drug delivery properties. These inherent qualities make alginates a cut above the rest and can give a paradigm shift to the naturally extracted compounds in pharmaceutical field. The alginate is one of the biomaterials which possess advantageous properties and resemble structurally with matrix tissue and their properties are tuneable as per the prerequisites to serve the various roles in drug delivery (Kumar et al., 2024). Hence, alginates may provide a promising future to researchers in different biomedical field.

Alginates are clean alternative for future, as available in nature abundantly, biodegradable, biocompatible, commercially successful and environment friendly also (Hurtado et al., 2022). Due to the abundant availability of raw alginate in nature and its advantage of being easily recycled, alginate-based materials are conducive in shifting away from an economy draining fossil fuels to a more environmentally friendly and sustainable one (Zdiri et al., 2022). In addition, its inherent biocompatibility makes alginate a fascinating biomedical material (Kavand et al., 2024). Moreover, as compared to other polysaccharides, alginates can combine in both liquid and gel phase which makes more preferable over others (Draget et al., 1997).

Alginate is naturally occurring polysaccharide found in green, red and mostly brown algae, sea weeds and some bacteria (Mostolizadeh, 2024). Alginate is a canopy term used for alginic acid and its different derivates present. Alginates are anionic in nature with cations of sodium, potassium, calcium, barium, strontium, magnesium etc. (Kumar et al., 2024; Sahoo and Biswal, 2021). Alginate is a water-soluble polysaccharide formed by cross linking of β-1,4-D-mannuronic acid (M) and β-1,4-D-guluronic acid (G) respectively bonded by 1–4 glycosidic linkage resulting into the formations of diverse structure with versatile physicochemical properties. The different arrangement and ratio of the M and G monomeric units is responsible for variation in properties of alginates (Hurtado et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2023). The alginate has different and complex structure depending upon the source from they have been extracted. The factors that alter the composition and structure of alginates are supposed to be because of polysaccharide chain length, glycosidic linkage, composition and sequence of monomeric units (Guo et al., 2020). Ratio of G and M in the polymer, significantly affects the properties like solubility, elasticity, gelation or strength (Sahoo and Biswal, 2021). The alginates containing higher content of G monomeric units are rigid and have higher strength as compared to that of containing M monomeric units. Polysaccharides rich in G monomers are more water soluble than that of M monomers rich polysaccharides. This sequence and richness of M or G units makes the alginates supple for their applications and usage in different domains of food, cosmetic and pharmaceutical industries with long shelf life (Cheng et al., 2020). Along with the sequence of G and M units, the physicochemical properties of alginates also depend upon molar mass, and M/G ratio. All these variations mentioned above contributes to the remodelling ability of alginate into different arrangements as per the requirement. The alginates are used as emulsifiers (Zheng et al., 2023), hydrogels (Savić Gajić et al., 2023), microcapsules (Gómez-Mascaraque et al., 2023), nanoforms (Iqbal et al., 2024b), fibres (Jiang et al., 2024) etc and also have diverse applications in medical field including, in drug delivery (Hariyadi and Islam, 2020), wound healing (Mahheidari et al., 2024), composites (Cao et al., 2023). Commercially, alginate extracted from different species of sea weeds including Laminaria, Macrocystis, Sargassum, Ascophyllum, Lessonia, Eclonia and Durvillea. Apart from the algae and sea weeds, alginates have also been extracted from Pseudomonas and Azotobacter bacterial strains (Deng et al., 2021; García-Astrain and Avérous, 2019). The Fig. 1 illustrates the diverse characteristics of alginate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability aspects of alginate for zeta potential
ce:section.ce:para: Alginate is a naturally occurring anionic polysaccharide derived primarily from brown seaweed, widely used in pharmaceutical and biomedical applications due to its biocompatibility, biodegradability, and gel-forming ability (Kumar et al., 2023). It comprises linear chains of α-L-guluronic (G) and β-D-mannuronic (M) acid residues, whose ratio affects its physicochemical properties. In terms of stability, zeta potential is a critical parameter for assessing alginate-based colloidal systems. Zeta potential measures the surface charge of particles, indicating their electrostatic repulsion and stability in suspension. For alginate nanoparticles or hydrogels, a zeta potential value of ± 30 mV or higher typically suggests good colloidal stability, minimizing particle aggregation (Wu et al., 2018). The negative surface charge of alginate, due to carboxylate groups, provides inherent electrostatic stabilization. However, factors such as pH, ionic strength, and crosslinking agents (e.g., calcium ions) can influence zeta potential, thus impacting stability. Maintaining appropriate zeta potential is essential for ensuring the effective performance and shelf-life of alginate-based drug delivery systems (Karim et al., 2022).

Even after tremendous success in medicinal field and best efforts made in last few decades to reverse or treat the brain disorders, the underlying complexity of brain is still a task at hand for researchers (Rodrigues et al., 2024). An untrodden mechanism and blood brain barrier systems of nervous system obstruct the effectiveness of conventional medicines in drug delivery. The current treatment of brain disorders success rate is very poor because of non-permeable architecture of blood brain barrier and slow drug delivery rate of classical drugs which leads to the ineffectiveness in curing neurological disorders (Ahlawat et al., 2020; Schwieger et al., 2020).

The firm barrier of the blood brain barrier was first traced by a German microbiologist Paul Ehrlich in 1885 and Edwin Goldman in 1913 while injecting an azo dye into the brain that could not delivered to brain due to a barrier (Zhou et al., 2018). To prevail over this barrier for drug delivery to the targeting areas of brain suffering by various disorders like Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, epilepsy and other brain disorders nanoformulations provides a promising pathway in drug delivery (Han and Jiang, 2021; Kakinen et al., 2024; Khaitan et al., 2018; Nguyen-Thi et al., 2024; Sa et al., 2022). There are three types of barriers which brings the drug delivery at halt, the blood brain barrier, the arachnoid barrier, and the blood-cerebrospinal fluid barrier (Anwarkhan et al., 2024). The blood brain interface is separated from the rest of the body by a biological membrane and is composed of distinct cells to regulate the passage of selective ions or molecules, hence protecting brain selectively supply the nutrients to the brain (Dotiwala et al., 2025).

The structural properties including shape, size, charge and surface properties are the variables which affect the efficacy of the nanomedicines for the treatment of the brain disorders. For efficient paracellular transportation and drug delivery of nano-formulations across the blood brain barrier, size of nanomedicines plays a key role in approaching the targeted site (Yousfan et al., 2024). In human nervous system drug delivery nanomedicines with 10–100 nm size are most effective. As sometimes, the nanoparticles much smaller than 10 nm and greater than 100 nm are usually accumulated in spleen and liver or excreted by renal system (Bourquin et al., 2018). The tight junctions of blood brain barrier do not allow the nanomedicines with size > 100 nm to pass through it. Studies indicates that the appropriate size of nanomedicines which can be transported through the natural blood barrier gatekeeper lies specifically between 10 to 100 nm (Khalin et al., 2022; Ohta et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2023).

From a huge number of studies conducted, it can be concluded that the transport of nano-formulations across the blood brain barrier is one of the key factors in the developments of nano drugs for the treatment of brain disorders (Betzer et al., 2017; Krsek et al., 2024). Spherical nano-formulations has been exploited in the transportation across the blood brain barrier (BBB). Although less exploited, other shapes of nano-formulations for example, circular, elliptical, elongated barrel shaped, nanorods etc., have also been synthesized (Joudeh and Linke, 2022). The geometry of nanoformulatuons is an important parameter of nanoformulations in drug delivery and has led the researchers to exploit the different shaped nanocarriers to prepare an effective class of compounds. Shape of nanoformulations directly alter their biodistribution and circulation duration (Hadji and Bouchemal, 2022). As compared to the other shapes, worm like nanoparticles have long duration of circulation and the non-rigidity of such nano worm or nanowires further promotes their transportation across the barrier. Due to higher biodistribution rates, these nano worms concentrate in the targeted areas and hence shows enhanced activity (Mishra et al., 2023). As compared to spherical nanoformulations, cellular uptake is more for nanorods and nano discs, which shows the importance of geometry of engineered nanoparticles in drug delivery (Banerjee et al., 2016). Although, thorough clinical trials are still required to overcome the challenges associated with shape like reliability, circulation and distribution to make non-spherical drugs approved medically (Mhatre and Sodha, 2019). The Fig. 2 illustrates the elimination of nanoformulations in body in major neurological disorders.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages of nanotechnology in brain targeting
ce:section.ce:para: For drug delivery into the brain blood brain barrier is the most challenged part of human body. The blood brain barrier has a distinctive property to selectively allow or not allow any foreign material to pass through it which makes it a great gatekeeper of brain but on the other hand, it also poses difficulty in drug delivery (Achar et al., 2021; Niazi, 2023; Pandit et al., 2020). To pass this stumbling block in brain disorder treatments, this is an ethical imperative and a responsibility which all researchers have been shouldering (Pinheiro et al., 2021; Saeedi et al., 2019). In this direction, nanotechnology has given good results in treatment of brain disorders without much ramification. Nanomedicine by amalgamating the science and technology has provided an essential tool to develop efficient drug delivery agents which work precisely with desired results and minimum side effects (Ayub and Wettig, 2022; Shi et al., 2017).

As the name indicates, nanomedicine is a collective term which embodies the different types of drugs with nano scale dimension (Yang et al., 2022). Nanomedicines has overcome the major drawbacks of conventional drugs being used in brain disorder treatments and have become a ground-breaking means to serve as an efficient drug carrier across the blood brain barrier (Gao, 2016). With the help of nanotechnology, it is now possible to deliver a drug to a specific location with chosen amount of dose and at right time to reap maximum benefit without damaging the neighbouring tissues (Jovčevska, 2022). The ability of nanoformulations to release drug in targeted areas in a controlled manner makes them a reliable and effective option for diseases like Alzheimer’s, brain tumours, Parkinson’s disease, schizophrenia, depression (Nair et al., 2023). The growing impact of nanomedicines is due to their comparable size to cellular systems hence, can easily pass the natural blood brain barrier firewall, non-toxic nature, target specific, unique surface characteristics and ability for long circulation (Ljubimov et al., 2022; Shrimal et al., 2020).

Blood brain barrier (BBB) represents demarcation between the brain and the rest of body which protects the brain to maintain the homeostasis with a drawback that it also hinders the study to develop novel drugs to treat the brain disorders (Tan et al., 2022). Nanoformulations possess a wide variety of formations, each having unique properties and drug delivery capacity and pathway providing a number of options for the treatment of brain disorders and other diseases also (Khan et al., 2018; Ma et al., 2024; Sabir et al., 2022; Zeynalzadeh et al., 2024).

In last few decades the pervasiveness and fatality of brain disorders has increased drastically which has created an epidemic like conditions. In this situation of obfuscation, being a malleable class of nanodrugs, the lipid based nanoformulations have revolutionised the field of nanomedicines (Cai et al., 2024; Hou et al., 2021). Commercially lipid based nanoformulations have played an important role in drug delivery for Pfizer and Moderna novel against COVID-19 virus (Wilson and Geetha, 2022). Broadly speaking, key types of lipid-based nanoformulations includes liposomes, nanoemulsions, solid-lipid nanoformulations, nano-structured lipid carriers and lipid-polymer hybrid form (Alfutaimani et al., 2024).

SLNs are considered as reputed class of nanovehicles to carry drugs across the blood brain barrier to locate the target site specifically and efficiently (Dara et al., 2019; Gordillo-Galeano and Mora-Huertas, 2018; Kambale et al., 2022). SLNs are hydrophobic molecules with lipid having diameter in the range of 50–1000 nm. Reduced toxicity, controlled drug release, long circulation up to a duration of five days, target specific properties of SLNs make them beneficial in the field of nano drug delivery (Froelich et al., 2021; Poudel and Park, 2022; Subodh et al., 2024). A number of biogenic synthesis and applications of SLNs have been reported by using flavonoids (quercetin), compritol 888, diterpenoid (Andrographolide), chitosan and other bioactive compounds (ferulic acid) against brain disorders like Alzheimer’ disease and sustained drug delivery across the blood brain delivery (Graverini et al., 2018; Pandian et al., 2021; Pinheiro et al., 2020; Rassu et al., 2017; Saini et al., 2021; Suchita et al., 2025). It can be concluded the number of studies reported, solid-lipid nanoformulations have become a popular nano drug vehicle as they own several advantages over other types of solid lipid nanoparticles which has overcome the failings of drug delivery across the impermeable brain barrier.

Nanoemulsions are heterogenous and thermodynamically stable systems of two immiscible liquids with size range in 20 to 200 nm in the form of transparent or translucent nanodroplets. The two immiscible liquids may form emulsion either as oil in water (O/W) or water in oil (W/O) or double emulsion (W/O/W & O/W/O) which makes them to carry both hydrophilic and hydrophobic drugs (Sharma et al., 2025). Even though nanoemulsions have high drug solubility and highly active in drug delivery across the blood brain barrier, but are less stable (Bonferoni et al., 2019; Nair et al., 2023). As the nanoemulsions are stabilized by Brownian movement, their stability can further be increased by adding the particular surfactants (A et al., 2021). Nanoemulsions have grabbed the segment of market of nanodrugs as compared to the other nanovehicles as they provide an easy manufacturing and convenient methods of drug delivery (Eqbal et al., 2021; Gupta et al., 2019; Roy et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2019). These are isotropic biphasic nanoemulsions have broad spectrum of applications due to their bioavailability and multiple routes of drug delivery like oral (Desai and Thakkar, 2019), topical (Krishnan and Mitragotri, 2020), intranasal (Pardeshi and Belgamwar, 2018; Shobo et al., 2018), pulmonary (Arbain et al., 2019) and ocular (Fang et al., 2025; Nair et al., 2023) which have contributed a major role in drug delivery which not confined to conventional drug delivery with poor results (Nirale et al., 2020). Moreover, nanoemulsion form on stabilization on surfactants and co-surfactants enabled them in a way that these can utilised in drug delivery in the form of gels (Bayanati et al., 2021), spray (Nair et al., 2021), aerosols and drops (Mohd Nordin et al., 2021). The research findings advocate for the commercial applicability (Alaayedi and Maraie, 2023; Alhakamy et al., 2024; Bonferoni et al., 2019; Kaur et al., 2022; Nemade et al., 2022; Pires et al., 2022; Usama Ashhar et al., 2022; Wilar et al., 2024) of nanoemulsions in drug delivery across the gate keeper of brain, that’s why nanoemulsions are considered as unique class of nanocargos (Ahmed et al., 2022; Cunha et al., 2021; Dogra et al., 2024; Faria et al., 2024; Gao et al., 2025; Guo et al., 2023a; Hussein et al., 2017; Iqbal et al., 2024a; Kumar et al., 2022; Meirinho et al.; Prakash et al., 2023; So et al., 2025; Tiwari et al., 2025).

To some extent nanoformlautions have been proved to be effective in combating with drug delivery in neuro disorders and can become a life changing therapeutic involvement. In this field, polymeric nanoparticles hold a special place in revolutionising the cure for neurodegenerative diseases (Annu et al., 2022; Cano et al., 2020). Other properties that make them more faithful because of their in vivo stability, easy to synthesize and are good carriers for drug delivery (Annu et al., 2022). When talking about the nano vehicles that can cross the blood brain barrier without much hurdle and hustle, polymeric nanoformulations offers a number of benefits including bioavailability, controlled drug release and excellent drug loading capacity. Amongst all, polymeric nanoformulations, poly (butylcyanoacrylate) (PBCA), PLA, PLGA and chitosan based polymeric nanoformulations have shown promising results (Moreira et al., 2024).

In late 1970 s, biodegradable fluorescein loaded PBCA ‘nanocapsules’ were synthesized by Patrick Couvreur with promising outcome in drug delivery at nano level which made PBCA nanoparticles a promising tool for drug delivery (Vauthier, 2019; Wenqi et al., 2024). As per the studies conducted, polysorbate 80 coated poly (butylcyanoacrylate) (PBCA) nanoformulations have been used by researchers as an effective and biodegradable nanocarrier for brain targeting. The nano-formulations were found to impersonate the lipoproteins and interacted with epithelial cells of brain hence acted as an anchor in transferring the drugs across the brain barrier. Poly (butylcyanoacrylate) (PBCA) based nanoformulations coated with polysorbate 80 can incorporate small drugs, peptides and proteins as well. The polysorbate 80 coating camouflages the lipoprotein and facilitates to pass the blood brain barrier (Wilson, 2009). PBCA polymers using n-butyl-cyanoacrylate (BCA) monomeric units used as a tissue adhesive have been prepared by high reproducible mini-emulsion preparation method resulted into high quality drug shipper (Ghaferi et al., 2020; Hassanzadeganroudsari et al., 2020; Kolter et al., 2015; Salehi et al., 2024; Thammasit et al., 2021).

Although, synthesis of nanoformulations to deal with the easy passage through blood brain barrier with less cytotoxicity is an unnerving task to date (Chacko and Jose, 2023). In connection with, PLGA nanoformulations are one of the favourite polymers to researchers. As these are capable of encapusating both hydrophobic and hydrophilic drugs (Guo et al., 2023b). Theses also assure safety to the body as on breakdown glycolic acid and lactic acid are formed as end products. Different methods including precipitation, spray drying, double-emulsion solvent evaporation and low temperature spray extraction have been employed to prepare PLGA nanoformulations (Zhi et al., 2021). A plenty of recent studies has been dedicated to synthesize poly lactide-co-glycolide (PLGA) nanoformulations to succeed in coming up epidemic of brain disorders which have paved recently due to many lifestyle changes in last few decades (Anand et al., 2022; Pinto et al., 2022). The novel PEGylated nanoformulations have been synthesized and characterized, with different length and density of PEG chain with varying circulation time, persistence and biodistribution of nanoformulations (Katila et al., 2022; Monge et al., 2020; Rabanel et al., 2020). The Table 1 illustrates different types and applications of diverse polymeric nanoformulations.

In the quest of sensitive and cost effective nanoformulations, quantum dots (QDots) represent novel and excellent nanomaterials with size ranging from 2-10 nm (Haleem et al., 2023). Their inimitable properties have drawn the attention of researchers in last many years and are considered as best possible option as nanocarriers and nanoprobes (Subham Jain et al., 2022). To get the desired fate of the nanovehicles in biological systems, QDots provide a sensitive and real time observer because of the inherent properties like small size, exceptional surface chemistry, microenvironment sensitivity, high brightness and photostability (Ghaderi et al., 2011; Gidwani et al., 2021; Jana and Dev, 2022; Probst et al., 2013). To address the conventional pharamacological drawbacks novel QDots have been synthesized by researchers to improve drug delivery systems at nanolevel. Such promising nanocarriers with outstanding optical and surface chemistry have been reported by researchers (Linkov et al., 2016).

As carbon based QDots are trending in medical field, as they are abundant in nature and economic as well (Nair et al., 2020). High yield green methods haven been employed to synthesize blue and green carbon dots merely by altering the reaction time. These dots shown remarkable applications in bioimaging and as chemical sensors. For last few years, immense research is being carried out in all domains of science to harness the concealed potential of QDots (Chang et al., 2017; Prathumsuwan et al., 2018).

With the evolvement of nanoformulations in drug delivery, lot of innovative nanoformulations with different composition and functions have been designed (Krsek et al., 2024; Kurul et al., 2025; Yusuf et al., 2023). In this field a substantial number of efforts have been made to synthesized metal based nanoformulations (Mitchell et al., 2021). To design smart nano cargos across the blood brain barrier, metal based nanoformulations provides a variety of provisions to compensate the drawbacks of other types of nanoformulations (Formica et al., 2022). These include, specifically metals, metal oxides or semiconductors with tailor made properties (Meena et al., 2020) like shape, size, structure, composition with a safe, biocompatible and stable nano compositions (Ahmed et al., 2024; Burdușel et al., 2018; Persano et al., 2021; Teleanu et al., 2019). The most commonly explored inorganic nanoformulations are gold, silver, iron, cerium, silica graphene etc. with modifiable shapes i.e. sphere, rod, prisms etc (Jiao et al., 2018; Kowalska et al., 2024; Martano et al., 2022). Their numerous applications in the different fields of treatment and diagnosis have made them a faithful treatment system on which the coming generation can rely on. The inorganic nanoformulations have potential to slow down the irreversible changes caused by different neurodegenerative disorders (Gallardo-Toledo et al., 2021; Todorova et al., 2023). The Fig. 5 summarise the different route of administration along with various types of nanoformulations.

Since ancient times, silver nanoparticles are being used as antimicrobial agents in different forms (Mathur et al., 2018). The surface chemistry, size, shape, electrical and catalytic properties of silver nanoparticle are easily tuneable that’s why they can be used in different areas of drug delivery (Vishveshwaraiah et al., 2025). Now, with advancements in research, many studies shown that the silver nanoformulations scan overcome the formidable barrier to brain easily hence are considered as a great nano-vehicles (Usha et al., 2025). Other than conventional uses, silver nanoformulations have been used in suppressing the brain tumours and side effects of drugs (Tunç, 2024). Lately, the potential of silver nanoformulations have been explored their efficacy against less efficient treatments like surgery and chemotherapy in brain cancer therapy (Naveed et al., 2024; Xu et al., 2020).

Other metal in nanoform which has been extensively used in drug delivery across the blood brain barrier are gold nanoparticles (Tapia-Arellano et al., 2024). Gold nanoparticles have surmounted the drawbacks of conventional methods and barriers of drug delivery by providing a target specific drug delivery (Chiang et al., 2024; Qureshi et al., 2024). To avoid an open brain surgery without displaying any side effects to the other organs of the body. Gold nano stars have been synthesized by using infra-red radiations replacing the X-ray and chemotherapy which showed an excellent targeting capacity inside the brain (Arami et al., 2022). Moreover, silver and gold nanoformulations could conjoined to different ligands and biological molecules which enhances the target specificity of these nanoformulations towards most incapacitating neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer’s disease and Parkinson’s disease (Ribeiro et al., 2022; Scarpa et al., 2023). To provide novel and effective therapies to counter with devastating brain dysfunctions, nanoformulations of metal oxides have also helped to reverse or slowed down the occurrence of these diseases. Owing to their physicochemical properties, metal oxide nanoformulations have stimulated a way to biocompatible, economic treatment of brain disorders (Alphandéry, 2020; Valentina et al., 2017). Among all medicinal nano materials, magnetic iron oxide nanoformulations of iorn, nickel, copper and cobalt have been studied extensively by researchers (Ansari et al., 2019; Gul et al., 2021; Marouzi et al., 2021; Rajeshkumar et al., 2019). Supermagnetism and ideal compatibility of nano iron oxides especially wustite, magnetite, hematite and maghemite have been explored the most (Ajinkya et al., 2020). The metal oxide nanoformulations accumulates in the targeted areas by attaching themselves to the affected cells hence boosting the target specificity and effectiveness of the nanomedicines at very low doses (Kimta et al., 2025; Parvez et al., 2022; Ratiram et al., 2019; Yassin et al., 2024).
The Table 2 depicts the properties and applications of different nanoformulations.

The exemplary properties of alginates play a very important role in customizing the properties of nanodrugs to fabricate them successfully into drug delivery systems. With a robust and improved mechanical and biomedical properties, nanoforms of alginates have become a platform to contest the synthetic nanoformulations (Qamar et al., 2024). Alginate based nanodrugs possess versatile and unique physicochemical properties including good drug loading capacity, high cellular uptake, controlled drug release etc. As the alginates based nanoformulations are modifiable and easy going when their properties come to custom make as per the need of hour. They can be incorporated as hydrogels, porous frameworks, nanovehicles or carriers. The alginates based nanoformulations has offered an imperishable future to medicinal field (Iravani and Varma, 2022). The synergic effect of alginates with metal nanoformulations have enhanced the clinical potential of drugs in tumour growth inhibition (Mirrahimi et al., 2020). The sequence of monomeric units of alginates provides an easy pathway in the incorporation of alginates in different types of nanodrugs to carry out the important enzymatic reactions and their implementation as biodegradable nanodrug carriers (Zia et al., 2017). Furthermore, alginate nanoformulations can mimic the extracellular tissues due to gel forming capacity which makes it a biocompatible and reliable drug delivery payload (Abourehab et al., 2022; Hasnain and Nayak, 2018). To sum up, alginate based nanoformlulations are biodegradable, easily bendable, non-toxic, renewable guaranteeing a safe and effective drug delivery system platform in future to develop a better solution for sustainable nanodrugs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of alginate NPs for brain disorders
ce:section.ce:para: Alginate-based nanoparticles have emerged as promising carriers for brain-targeted drug delivery due to their biocompatibility, biodegradability, and ease of surface modification. The fundamental mechanism by which alginate NPs treat brain disorders involves crossing the blood–brain barrier (BBB), a highly selective membrane that restricts drug access to the brain (Kruczkowska et al., 2025). Alginate NPs typically range from 10 to 200 nm, an optimal size range that facilitates endocytosis or transcytosis through endothelial cells lining the BBB. The surface modification with targeting ligands such as transferrin, lactoferrin, or peptides enhances receptor-mediated transport across the BBB, improving site-specific delivery to affected brain regions (Mohammadbaghban et al., 2024).

Once across the BBB, alginate NPs achieve therapeutic efficacy via sustained and controlled drug release. The ionically crosslinked structure of alginate enables pH-sensitive or enzyme-responsive behavior, allowing the NPs to release their cargo in response to the unique microenvironment of diseased brain tissue. This controlled release reduces systemic side effects and maintains therapeutic drug concentrations at the site of action over extended periods (Abbasi et al., 2023).

Alginate NPs can encapsulate a wide range of therapeutic agents including small molecules, proteins, antioxidants, and nucleic acids. In neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s, they have been shown to reduce oxidative stress, inhibit neuroinflammation, and enhance neuronal survival. For brain tumors, alginate NPs can facilitate intracellular delivery of chemotherapeutic agents, increasing cytotoxicity to tumor cells while sparing healthy tissue (Khursheed et al., 2020). The Fig. 6 illustrates the mechanistic insights into alginate nanoparticles for brain disorder management.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Targeting strategies of alginate NPs for brain disorders
ce:section.ce:para:
Alginate nanoparticles have been engineered with various targeting strategies to enhance drug delivery to the brain. One key approach involves surface functionalization (Del Amo et al., 2021) with ligands such as transferrin, lactoferrin, or folic acid, which exploit receptor-mediated transcytosis across the BBB. These ligands bind to specific receptors overexpressed on brain endothelial cells, enhancing selective uptake. Another promising strategy is the use of cell-penetrating peptides (Wu and Angelova, 2023) like TAT peptide, which facilitate the internalization of NPs into neuronal cells. Magnetic targeting (Rostami, 2022), where alginate NPs are combined with superparamagnetic iron oxide, enables the use of external magnetic fields to direct drug-loaded NPs to specific brain regions. Additionally, stimuli-responsive systems, such as pH- or redox-sensitive alginate NPs, enable on-demand drug release in diseased microenvironments. These multifunctional targeting strategies significantly improve therapeutic efficacy and minimize off-target effects, making alginate NPs a potent platform for treating brain disorders (Hu et al., 2024). The targeted delivery approaches using alginate nanoparticles for brain disorders is illustrated in Fig. 7
.

The highly protected nature of the central nervous system (CNS) makes diagnosing and treating brain disorders a significant challenge (Thau et al., 2019). The blood–brain barrier inhibits the macromolecule from entering the brain. The challenging anatomic accessibility and diagnostics make conventional drugs ineffective in brain disorders (Ale and Nainwal, 2023). However, compared to these conventional micro-meter scale drugs, nanomaterials precisely mimic the surface characteristics of organic tissues, including topography (variations in structure) and surface energy which influence cellular behaviours like adhesion, proliferation and differentiation (Habibzadeh et al., 2022; Kladko et al., 2021). The wide range of features of nanoformulations includes a high ratio of surface area to volume, site-specific targeting, controlled drug release, adaptability in facilitating surface modifications, and multifunctionality (Shabani et al., 2023).

Nanotechnology exists at the juncture of science and technology. It is now widely regarded as a holistic approach for addressing many present-day challenges, such as environmental pollution, food spoilage, waste, and health-related conditions (He et al., 2019). Moreover, nanotechnology has a wide range of applications and can create significant social and economic impacts by boosting efficiency, reducing cost, and substantially improving the quality of human life (Mohapatra et al., 2024). Lately, alginate-based nanostructures have drawn significant attention from industry and academia due to their excellent biodegradability and biocompatibility, making them a safe choice for medical applications. Alginate-based nanoparticles are utilized in various applications, including wound dressing, cancer therapy, tissue engineering, drug and gene delivery, antimicrobial products, and biosensors. Thus, alginate is being frequently used to fabricate nanocapsules, nanoparticles, nanocoating and nanostructured scaffolds, membranes, beads, gels, and sponge forms. The promising features of alginate-based nanoparticles show significant promise in addressing brain disorders (Dodero et al., 2021) Table 3 .

Currently, the pharmacological treatment for Alzheimer's disease primarily involves the use of N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor antagonists and acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibitors. AChE inhibitors like galantamine, rivastigmine, and donepezil increases the cholinergic transmission in the synaptic cleft. However, only mild to moderate Alzheimer’s disease can be treated with these drugs. Another drug, memantine is commonly used to treat moderate to severe Alzheimer’s disease (Pinto et al., 2022).

To address the limitations of current therapy and to improve the therapeutic outcomes of drugs delivery numerous alginate-based nanoformulations have been developed. For instance, catechin was encapsulated in chitosan-alginate nanoparticles to transport catechin to the rat's brain to treat AlCl3-induced Alzheimer’s disease. The findings revealed that a large amount of drug was released from the nanoparticles at pH 7 in an in vitro drug release study. Since the pH of blood circulation is 7 which shows that after entering into the blood a large part of the drug gets released from the nanoparticles and if the larger amount of the drug remains in the blood, the larger part of it will get transported to the brain. Moreover, the in vivo study results showed that the AChE levels were reduced in the group treated with nanoparticles compared to the untreated group. However, no significant difference was found in AChE levels in groups treated with catechin and catechin-loaded nanoparticles. The nanoparticles administered for 21 days orally also improved learning and memory in AlCl3-induced Alzheimer’s disease rats. The study suggests that catechin-loaded chitosan alginate nanoparticles treat Alzheimer’s disease in rats by reducing AChE activity and improving cognitive function (Mohammadbaghban et al., 2024). Oxidative stress contributes to Alzheimer’s disease by promoting amyloid plaque formation, tau protein hyperphosphorylation, and the loss of synapses and neurons. This indicates that oxidative stress is an important part of Alzheimer’s disease pathology and antioxidants may be beneficial for treating Alzheimer’s disease. Curcumin obtained from Curcuma longa shows significant antioxidant activity by inhibiting the formation of various reactive oxygen species.

Yekeler et al., 2024 prepared curcumin-loaded PLGA nanoparticle-embedded sodium alginate/gelatin 3D-printed scaffolds for treating Alzheimer’s disease. The 3-D printed scaffolds dissolved in a simulated salivary fluid wetted in less than 1 s and completely dispersed and dissolved in 2.30 s in an in vitro study. These findings align with the recommendations that orodispersable formulations should disintegrate in less than 30 s. Moreover, the 3-D printed scaffolds were evaluated using Aβ1-42 peptide (20 µM) induced SH-SY5Y AD cells at 1, 10 and 20 µM concentrations. The findings revealed cell viability reduced to 28.2 % in Alzheimer’s disease-induced cells. However, cells treated with 3-D scaffolds retained their cell viability with the best viability observed at the least concentration of 1 µM. This suggests that these 3-D printed scaffolds show promising effects in in vitro neuroprotective assay. Moreover, they should be further examined for its sublingual administration using in vivo animal studies and clinical trials (Yekeler et al., 2024).

Parkinson’s disease is a chronic progressive neurodegenerative disorder marked by the early and significant loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta (SNpc) and the widespread accumulation of an intracellular protein alpha-synuclein (aSyn). The classical motor symptoms of Parkinson’s disease including rigidity, tremor, bradykinesia, and postural instability are caused by dopamine deficiency in the basal ganglia (Radhakrishnan and Goyal, 2018). The current management of Parkinson’s disease is dependent on pharmacological therapy. However, symptomatic drug treatments have key limitations in this advanced disease. Thus, developing novel medicines that could treat advanced Parkinson’s disease is crucial. Levodopa is a precursor of dopamine frequently used to treat Parkinson’s disease. However, the administration of levodopa in its oral form shows some disadvantages including toxicity, low bioavailability, and untargeted delivery.

Athira et al. have developed a novel dopamine-encapsulated ligno-alginate hydrogel coated with Fe3O4 and graphene oxide nanoparticles for treating Parkinson’s disease. Drug delivery performance was significantly improved with these nanoparticles. The study observed a 57.14–69 % substantial drug load coupled with a sustained release of 24–28 % for an extended period of 48 h. Moreover, in MTT cell viability assay the dopamine-encapsulated ligno-alginate nanocomposite demonstrated negligible effects on cell viability revealing the biocompatible nature of the composite. This study suggests that these nanocomposites possess low toxicity due to the slower release of levodopa from the carrier making it safer for drug application (K et al., 2024).

Likewise, Khanam et al. prepared a cabergoline alginate nanocomposite for treating Parkinson’s disease-induced flies. Another drug Cabergoline is a potent ergot-derived dopamine agonist used for the treatment of Parkinson’s disease. The nanocomposite at 1, 2, and 3 µM concentrations increased the climbing ability and reduced the oxidative stress in the brain dose-dependently when administered for 24 days by Parkinson’s disease-induced flies. This study concludes that the cabergoline alginate nanocomposite could decrease the side effects associated with the excessive use or higher dose of the drug and the desirable results could be obtained at lower doses with alginate nanocomposites (Khanam et al., 2018). Similarly, bromocriptine is a semi-synthetic ergot alkaloid used as a dopamine agonist to treat Parkinson’s disease but possesses lower bioavailability. A study investigated the effectiveness of bromocriptine alginate nanocomposite in the Parkinson’s disease-induced transgenic drosophila model. The Parkinson’s disease-induced flies were exposed to different concentrations (0.5, 1, and 1.5 µM) of nanocomposite for 24 days. Findings revealed a significant dose-dependent delay in losing the climbing ability of Parkinson’s disease-induced flies in nanocomposite-treated groups. Moreover, a dose-dependent reduction in brain oxidative stress was also observed in Parkinson’s disease-induced flies treated with the nanocomposite. This reduction and the delay in the loss of climbing ability were more profound in Parkinson’s disease-induced flies treated with bromocriptine nanocomposite than the bromocriptine alone. This indicates that the novel nanostructured bromocriptine alginate nanocomposite can be used in lower doses to get the desired therapeutic effect against Parkinson’s disease, thereby reducing the risk of side effects (Siddique et al., 2016).

Siddique et al. synthesized alginate nano-curcumin nanocomposite for treating Parkinson’s disease in transgenic drosophila. A presynaptic protein alpha-synuclein is expressed at the synaptic terminals in the CNS. The monomeric form of this protein can misfold and aggregate into larger oligomeric-fibrillar forms which are neurotoxic species (Lewy bodies). As a result, the dopaminergic neurons are lost leading to behavioral abnormalities. Moreover, oxidative stress and the generation of free radicals are other major reasons behind the degeneration of dopaminergic neurons. The formation of these neurotoxic species and the generation of reactive oxygen species are correlated with the onset of Parkinson’s disease. The progeny expresses the h-αSinuclein in the neurons and the flies are called Parkinson’s disease-induced flies. The alginate-curcumin nanocomposite showed protective effects against the progression of Parkinson’s disease symptoms in transgenic Parkinson’s disease-induced flies. The nanocomposite-treated group at the concentration of 10-5 g/ml markedly delayed the loss of the climbing ability of Parkinson’s disease-induced flies as compared to the control-treated group. Oxidative stress and apoptosis in the brain were also reduced in the nanocomposite-treated group. This suggests that alginate-curcumin nanocomposite can be developed as a novel drug with increased half-lives, solubility, and stability after scrutinizing it through in-depth pre-clinical studies (Siddique et al., 2013).

Epilepsy is the second most common neurological disorder affecting around 70 million people worldwide. It is characterized by seizures that are not predictable in frequency (Movahedpour et al., 2023; Roustaei et al., 2023). At present, no treatment is available to cure epilepsy completely and the drugs available in the market are only to control symptoms and decrease the frequency and severity of seizures. Moreover, the available medications like lamotrigine, and carbamazepine when administered systemically affect other organs producing side effects other than their therapeutic effects (Movahedpour et al., 2023). Thus, rational drug therapy more importantly targeted drug therapy is essential to circumvent these downsides. Excitotoxicity a part of epileptic brain damage is the process of neuronal injury by excessive activation of amino acid receptors. Oxidative stress plays a key role in it. Kunjumon et al. synthesized madecassoside-encapsulated alginate-chitosan nanoparticles to treat brain excitotoxicity. Madecassoside is a pentacyclic triterpenoid saponin best used for its memory-enhancing and neuroprotective properties. No toxicity in mouse normal neural stem cells was observed when treated with nanoparticles in cell viability assay. The effectiveness of these nanoparticles at the dose of 20 mg/Kg, iv; was evaluated using pilocarpine-induced rodent (mice) seizure model. Findings revealed that nanoparticles displayed significant permeability to the brain while the permeability was limited in groups treated with madecassoside alone. Additionally, nanoparticles reduced the number of animals experiencing the most severe seizures. These results suggest increasing aqueous solubility and transportation of nanoparticles to the brain demonstrate its increased bioavailability and biodistribution across the brain (Kunjumon et al., 2021). Another study revealed that curcumin-loaded chitosan-alginate-sodium tripolyphosphate (STPP) nanoparticles ameliorate memory deficits in pentylene tetrazole-induced kindling model of epilepsy in NMRI mice. Nanoparticles at 12.5 and 25 mg/Kg doses when administered for 10 days, i.p., showed significant anticonvulsant activity and inhibited cognitive impairment evaluated via the Morris water maze test. Moreover, mice treated with curcumin nanoparticles displayed reduced cell death and glial activation compared to those treated with curcumin alone. The study suggests hydrophobic curcumin's solubility can be enhanced via encapsulation with biodegradable polymers like chitosan and alginate (Hashemian et al., 2017).

A brain tumour is an abnormal and uncontrolled growth of brain cells. An unexpected growth within the brain can impact human functioning, as the skull is a rigid, volume-restricted structure, with effects varying based on the specific brain region involved (Kaifi, 2023). Brain tumours are mainly classified into two types: primary brain tumours (which start in the brain) and secondary brain tumours (which are formed by cancer cells migrated from other parts of the body to the brain). Glioma is the most common type of primary brain tumour, representing approximately 80 % of all cases (Qiu et al., 2022). Neurons are surrounded by glial cells which are responsible for maintaining their environment. Glioma is a malignant brain tumour with high-risk factors due to the lower permeability and poor uptake of drugs by the desired cells. The introduction of nanoformulations has surpassed these drawbacks in scientific studies. A study evaluated the effect of asiaticoside-encapsulated alginate chitosan nanoparticles at 20, 50, 100, and 200 µg/ml concentrations in C6 glioma cells. Findings revealed dose-dependent cytotoxicity in C6 glioma cells treated with nanoparticles. Moreover, late apoptosis and necrosis in glioma cells were observed alongside augmentation of reactive oxygen species generation. The study indicates that asiaticoside nanoparticles act as an antiproliferative agent for glioma cells through an enhanced intracellular ROS pathway that promotes necrosis/apoptosis (Kunjumon et al., 2021). Mousavi et al. synthesized SPIO@AuNP-cisplatin-alginate nano-complex (SACA) consisting of a SPIO core, a gold shell, and an alginate coating. SACA at 5 and 30 µg/ml concentrations, when incubated for 4 h, reduced the cell viability of U87MG human glioblastoma cells in MTT assay. The reduction was greater than in the cells treated with cisplatin alone. However, in vivo experiments are required to prove the effectiveness of these nanoparticles in brain tumours (Mousavi et al., 2023).

Zhao et al. (Zhao et al., 2021) investigated the impact of alginate-TiO2 temozolomide (TMZ) nanoparticles on the pathology of neuroblastoma. A model of neuroblastoma was developed utilizing neuroblastoma cells, and alginate-TiO2 TMZ nanoparticles were synthesized through the aerosolization of low-viscosity sodium alginate (250–360 kDa). After a duration of 28 days of intervention, the proliferation of neuroblastoma was effectively regulated within the TTG and ATTG cohorts ( Fig. 8
a). In contrast, the proliferation was comparatively less effectively managed in the TG cohorts. The combination of alginate and TiO2 exhibited a synergistic effect in the suppression of neuroblastoma progression. The alginate-TiO2 nanoparticles retarded the release of TMZ, thereby maintaining the pharmacological efficacy over an extended period and augmenting the therapeutic outcomes. TiO2-TMZ nanoparticles significantly enhanced the survival rate of neuroblastoma murine models in the TTG cohort in comparison to the CG cohort. The murine subjects in the CG cohort exhibited complete mortality within a span of 10 weeks post-animal grouping ( Fig. 8 b). The incorporation of alginate further augmented the survival rate of the models in the ATTG cohort relative to that observed in the TTG cohort. The findings indicated that the synergistic effect of the combined nanoparticles substantially improved the survival rate of neuroblastoma murine models to the utmost degree.

Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a rapidly progressing and fatal neurodegenerative disease. Presently, ALS is regarded as incurable and the only approved treatment, riluzole has a mild effect on survival time. Ahmadi et al., accessed the efficacy and safety of nano curcumin as add-on therapy to riluzole in patients with ALS. A 12-month, double-blind randomized, placebo control trial was conducted where 54 patients received either nano curcumin at 80 mg/Kg dose or a placebo in a 1:1 ratio. Completion of the study revealed a significant increase in the survival rate of patients treated with nano curcumin compared to those treated with placebo. The findings indicate that nano curcumin is safe and may enhance survival rates when used as an adjunct treatment for ALS patients (Ahmadi et al., 2018).

Hashemian et al. (Hashemian et al., 2017) investigated the efficacy of Curcumin-loaded chitosan-alginate-STPP nanoparticles in mitigating cognitive impairments and diminishing glial activation within the pentylenetetrazol-induced kindling model of epilepsy. Male NMRI mice were administered daily injections of curcumin nanoparticles at dosages of 12.5 or 25 mg/kg. All interventions were administered via the intraperitoneal route for a duration of 10 days prior to the administration of PTZ, with the injections continuing until one hour before each PTZ injection. The quantification of GFAP staining data further elucidated that the administration of curcumin nanoparticles results in a statistically significant decrease in the quantity of GFAP-positive cells within the CA3 and CA1 regions of the hippocampus when juxtaposed with the vehicle control group. Nevertheless, in comparison to the vehicle group, a high dosage of free curcumin supplementation exhibited a reduction in the quantity of activated astrocytes; however, this observation did not achieve statistical significance (CA3: 601.5 ± 22.02 in vehicle + PTZ and 338.4 ± 29.61 in curcumin-loaded NPs (25 mg/kg); CA1: 1129 ± 23.35 in vehicle + PTZ and 787.3 ± 18.85 in curcumin-loaded NPs (25 mg/kg) ( Fig. 9
A, B).

Alginate-based nanoformulations offer a promising approach for TBI by enhancing drug delivery across the blood–brain barrier, reducing inflammation, and promoting neuronal regeneration. Their biocompatibility, controlled drug release, and ability to encapsulate neuroprotective agents make them a valuable tool for improving therapeutic outcomes in TBI management.

Ma et al. (Ma et al., 2021) conducted a synthesis of a sodium alginate (SA)/collagen type I (Col)/SDF-1 hydrogel and examined the potential therapeutic efficacy of the SA/Col/SDF-1 hydrogel, which was incorporated with bone marrow-derived mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs), in the context of a traumatic brain injury (TBI) model. At 28 days post-transplantation, macroscopic morphological assessments indicated a notable decrease in the lesion area ( Fig. 10
A). The lesion volume observed in the BMSCs/SA/Col/SDF-1 cohort (4.8 ± 0.4 mm3) was significantly lesser than that recorded in the TBI group (13.6 ± 0.3 mm3), the BMSCs group (12.0 ± 0.6 mm3), and the BMSCs/SA/Col group (9.8 ± 0.8 mm3) as quantified by CV staining ( Fig. 10 B). Three days post-transplantation, the brain water content of the ipsilateral hemisphere in the BMSCs/SA/Col/SDF-1 cohort exhibited a statistically significant reduction when compared to the other three experimental groups ( Fig. 10 E); however, no notable differences were detected in the cerebellum across all four groups. Furthermore, the BMSCs/SA/Col/SDF-1 group (12.6 ± 1.3) demonstrated a markedly lower count of TUNEL-positive cells in contrast to the TBI group (64.2 ± 3.2), the BMSCs group (40.1 ± 1.9), and the BMSCs/SA/Col group (24.6 ± 2.0) ( Fig. 10 C and D), which was corroborated by an elevated expression of Bcl-2 and a diminished level of cleaved Caspase-3 and Bax ( Fig. 10 F and G). These results elucidated that the BMSCs/SA/Col/SDF-1 scaffold significantly mitigated brain edema, curtailed lesion volume, and suppressed neuronal apoptosis following TBI.

Chen et al. (Chen et al., 2023) conducted an assessment of three distinct hydrogel scaffolds and determined that the gelatin methacrylate (GelMA)/sodium alginate (Alg) (GelMA/Alg) hydrogel scaffold exhibited superior properties for the adherence, proliferation, and differentiation of neural stem cells (NSCs). In comparison to the control cohort (46.11 ± 5.772 %), the GelMA/Alg cohort (41.04 ± 12.00 %) exhibited a marginal decline, whereas all NSC-loaded GelMA/Alg cohorts, which include the GelMA/Alg + NSCs [0d] cohort (30.64 ± 7.907 %), GelMA/Alg + NSCs [7d] cohort (23.84 ± 3.876 %), and GelMA/Alg + NSCs [14d] cohort (32.06 ± 5.383 %), revealed a statistically significant decrease in tissue loss 28 days subsequent to traumatic brain injury (TBI), with the tissue loss rate in the GelMA/Alg + NSCs [7d] cohort being the most minimal ( Fig. 11
A-C). The controlled cortical impact (CCI) model resulted in a tissue defect of moderate magnitude, which was notably diminished following the application of the NSC-loaded GelMA/Alg hydrogel scaffold ( Fig. 11 A-C). The administration of GelMA/Alg in the absence of NSCs failed to elicit a neuroprotective effect in the context of TBI, as evidenced by the comparable beam-walking scores and asymmetry rates observed in the GelMA/Alg cohort relative to those documented in the control cohort ( Fig. 11 D and E). While the GelMA/Alg-loaded NSC demonstrated superior performance in the beam-walking assessment, the NSC [7d]-loaded GelMA/Alg cohort exhibited the most favorable outcome ( Fig. 11 D). The transplantation of the NSC [7d]-loaded GelMA/Alg hydrogel scaffold also resulted in markedly reduced asymmetric rates compared to the control group ( Fig. 11 E). The functional score derived from the beam-walking assessment exhibited a positive correlation with the rate of tissue loss observed on day 28 post-TBI, indicating that the beam-walking test may serve as a dependable metric for assessing tissue loss subsequent to TBI. These findings corroborated that the NSC [7d]-incorporated GelMA/Alg hydrogel scaffold provoked a sustained protective effect in the context of TBI.

The incorporation of alginate-derived nanoformulations in cerebral pharmacotherapy signifies a ground-breaking progression in the field of neurological medicine. Subsequent investigations ought to concentrate on refining the physicochemical characteristics of alginate-based nanocarriers to augment their capacity to traverse the BBB, all the while ensuring biocompatibility and safety. The investigation of hybrid nanocarriers, which amalgamate alginate with metallic nanoparticles, liposomes, or polymeric composites, may yield more effective drug delivery systems specifically designed for neurodegenerative disorders and intracranial tumors (Kruczkowska et al., 2025). Tailoring the physicochemical properties of alginate such as molecular weight, monomer composition (G/M ratio), and crosslinking density can significantly improve drug encapsulation efficiency, sustained release profiles, and stability in biological environments (Li et al., 2024).

Multifunctional hybrid systems represent a promising direction, where alginate is conjugated or co-formulated with other polymers (e.g., chitosan, PLGA), inorganic agents (e.g., magnetic nanoparticles, gold nanostars), or targeting ligands (e.g., transferrin, lactoferrin). These hybrid carriers can enhance blood–brain barrier (BBB) permeability, enable site-specific targeting, and facilitate real-time tracking via imaging agents (Jampílek et al., 2019). Furthermore, stimuli-responsive alginate-based nanocarriers, designed to react to environmental cues like pH, redox state, or enzymatic activity, offer great potential for fine-tuned and localized drug release within the central nervous system (CNS). The incorporation of artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) tools is anticipated to revolutionize formulation design. These technologies can assist in optimizing drug-to-polymer ratios, predicting biodistribution patterns, and customizing treatment regimens based on individual patient profiles (Aspatwar et al., 2024).

The convergence of alginate nanotechnology with stem cell therapy, gene editing (e.g., CRISPR-Cas9), and immunomodulatory strategies offers groundbreaking avenues for treating not only neurodegenerative conditions but also rare and previously intractable CNS diseases. By combining alginate’s favorable biological profile with next-generation biomedical technologies, future research can unlock robust, patient-centric, and disease-specific solutions in neurotherapeutics (Rasekh et al., 2025).

Personalized medicine represents an increasingly promising avenue for the application of alginate-based nanoformulations. Through the utilization of AI and ML, forthcoming research endeavours can establish predictive models aimed at tailoring alginate nanocarrier formulations to the unique needs of individual patients, thereby enhancing therapeutic efficacy while concurrently mitigating adverse effects (Jang et al., 2024). Furthermore, innovations in bioresponsive and stimuli-sensitive nanocarriers that exhibit responsiveness to variations in pH, temperature, or enzymatic activity will further enhance the precision of targeted drug delivery within the central nervous system. The enduring biocompatibility and possible immunogenic repercussions of alginate-derived nanodrugs necessitate comprehensive exploration through preclinical and clinical research trials. Extensive investigations pertaining to pharmacokinetics, biodistribution, and clearance mechanisms will be imperative in ascertaining the safety and regulatory endorsement of these innovative therapeutic agents. Furthermore, it is essential to establish scalable manufacturing methodologies to enable clinical applicability and commercial feasibility (Bernatoniene et al., 2025).

Despite the encouraging possibilities presented by alginate-derived nanoformulations for cerebral drug delivery, numerous obstacles impede their implementation in clinical settings. The foremost challenge involves surmounting the blood–brain barrier (BBB), which constrains drug permeability and limits the transport of therapeutic agents to specific brain regions. Furthermore, attaining meticulous regulation of drug release kinetics, stability, and bioavailability persists as a considerable concern. The biocompatibility and biodegradability of alginate-based carriers necessitate optimization to avert undesirable immune responses. The issues of scalability and economically viable production additionally complicate the prospect of large-scale application. In addition, extensive preclinical and clinical investigations are imperative to ascertain safety, efficacy, and adherence to regulatory standards for prospective therapeutic applications.

Alginate-based nanoformulations represent an innovative advancement in brain drug delivery, offering a biocompatible and efficient approach to overcoming the challenges posed by the blood–brain barrier. This review elucidates the distinctive characteristics of alginate, encompassing its biodegradability, minimal toxicity, and capacity to establish stable nanostructures, thereby rendering it a promising candidate for applications in neurology. The amalgamation of alginate with nanotechnology has exhibited considerable potential in the management of neurodegenerative disorders such as Alzheimer’s disease and Parkinson’s disease, in addition to afflictions including epilepsy, brain neoplasms, traumatic brain injury, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. A variety of alginate-based nanoformulations, comprising hydrogels, nanoparticles, and scaffolds, have demonstrated improved drug bioavailability, prolonged release, and targeted delivery to affected cerebral regions.

Notwithstanding these advancements, obstacles persist, such as the optimization of formulation stability, the enhancement of drug loading efficiency, and the assurance of clinical translation. Subsequent investigations ought to concentrate on the refinement of alginate-based nanocarriers, the improvement of their pharmacokinetic profiles, and the execution of thorough in vivo and clinical studies to substantiate their safety and efficacy. Through sustained innovation and interdisciplinary collaboration, alginate-based nanoformulations possess considerable potential to transform neurotherapeutics and to facilitate the development of more effective, patient-centered treatments for neurological disorders.

Ekta Rawat: Writing – original draft. Suman Sharma: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Shweta Vyas: Writing – review & editing, Software. Omar Awad Alsaidan: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Devesh U. Kapoor: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Bhupendra G. Prajapati: Writing – review & editing.
